title: Marmalade cake
subtitle: A loaf sponge with marmalade stirred in and brushed on top
excerpt: Marmalade cake uses thick-cut orange marmalade both inside the batter and brushed on the hot loaf as a glaze. The marmalade in the batter keeps the crumb moist and adds bitter orange peel through every slice. The glaze on top sets into a sticky, amber finish as the cake cools.
sourceNotes: Marmalade as a baking ingredient appears in British domestic cookery from the eighteenth century onward, when orange marmalade became a standard British preserve. The loaf cake form here follows standard British domestic baking convention from the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze glazed or unglazed. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps 4 days wrapped; flavour improves after 24 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, centre shelf; the marmalade can catch if baked too hot

content.text: Marmalade cake has orange marmalade stirred through the batter, giving the crumb a bitter citrus note and shreds of peel in every slice. The same marmalade is warmed and brushed over the loaf as soon as it leaves the oven, where it sets into a sticky, slightly glossy glaze. Use a thick-cut marmalade with plenty of peel; a fine-shred marmalade gives a paler result.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Beat the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well. Fold in the flour, then fold in the 100 g of marmalade. The batter will look slightly uneven because of the marmalade shreds; this is correct. Pour into the tin and bake for 50 to 55 minutes. The cake is done when a skewer through the centre comes out clean and the top is deep amber.

While the cake bakes, warm the remaining 3 tablespoons of marmalade in a small pan with a teaspoon of water until runny. As soon as the cake comes out of the oven, brush the glaze over the top generously. Leave to cool in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out, then cool fully on a wire rack.
